attributes.stem:
A 77-year old woman presents with a 6-month history of intermittent chest pain. She reports the pain to suddenly come on, with no obvious precipitant. She reports no dyspnoea, palpitations, lightheadedness, or syncope. However, over the same period, she has noticed associated dysphagia, which has steadily progressed from solids to liquids. She is a non-smoker, and reports no alcohol intake. She has had no recent voice hoarseness, unintentional weight loss or night sweats.

Her cardiovascular, respiratory and abdominal examinations are normal. Her ECG demonstrates no ischaemic changes or ST elevation. Subsequently, a barium contrast swallow is ordered (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This is a textbook appearance of the so-called 'corkscrew oesophagus' (interruptions of normal peristalsis with many non-propulsive, tertiary contractions in the distal oesophagus). The appearance and changes are characteristic of diffuse oesophageal spasm, although these only occur in <5% of cases. Diffuse oesophageal spasm is characterised by uncoordinated, high pressure contractions within the oesophagus, often leading to chest pain and a mistaken diagnosis of cardiac ischaemia.

Oesophageal achalasia results from a failure of organised oesophageal peristalsis. It characteristically involves short segments of the distal oesophagus. Classical barium swallow findings include the 'bird beak' or 'rat tail' (tapering of the distal oesophagus) signs.

Lower oesophageal carcinoma most commonly presents in males with a history of poorly controlled gastro-oesophageal reflux, who subsequently present with dysphagia (primarily for solids), odynophagia and constitutional symptoms (e.g. unintentional weight loss). Radiographic features include irregularly-shaped stricturing of the distal oesophagus but these can sometimes be subtle and in all cases of dysphagia, a gastroscopy is mandatory to exclude mucosal disease

Oesophageal leiomyomatosis is rare, benign condition associated with diffuse, abnormal proliferation of smooth muscle fibres in the distal oesophagus. It most commonly presents as slowly progressive dysphagia in female children. Radiographic features include distal oesophageal wall thickening, and tapered narrowing of distal oesophagus.

Oesophageal pseudodiverticulum is a rare condition, most commonly observed between the ages of 50 and 70 years, with a slight male predominance. On barium swallow, this condition commonly demonstrates numerous tiny flask-shaped outpouchings of oesophagus, which often appear to be 'floating' next to the oesophageal wall.
learningPoints: Diffuse oesophageal spasm has a classical (albeit rare) 'corkscrew oesophagus' appearance on barium swallow
Oesophageal achalasia is classically associated with the 'bird beak' sign (distal oesophageal tapering)
references: Roden DF, Altman KW. Causes of dysphagia among different age groups: a systematic review of the literature. Otolaryngologic Clinics of North America. 2013 Dec 1;46(6):965-87.
Spieker MR. Evaluating dysphagia. American Family Physician. 2000 Jun 15;61(12):3639-48.
